subsystem: HHVM (HipHop Virtual Machine) FastCGI server transport. FastCGITransport::onHeader is the per-parameter callback invoked by HHVM's FastCGI protocol parser as it decodes FCGI_PARAMS name-value pairs off the wire. Each call receives one decoded header/param name and value (as folly::IOBuf byte chains), converts them to std::string, and inserts the pair into the transport's m_requestParams map, which later populates request server variables (the $_SERVER-style CGI environment) seen by the executing web application.
provenance: Identified by the fully-qualified name FastCGITransport::onHeader, which is HHVM's runtime/server/fastcgi transport (facebook/hhvm). The folly::IOBuf type, folly::io::Cursor, readFixedString(), and computeChainDataLength() are Facebook's folly library primitives. The sample banner '/* sample: primevul_train_2071_vuln */' and filename 'corpus/train/train_0192_cwe665.cpp' indicate a PrimeVul-derived C++ sample tagged to CWE-665 (Improper Initialization / improper validation of decoded input).
trust_boundary: The trust boundary is the FastCGI wire protocol. key_chain and value_chain are produced by decoding FCGI_PARAMS records received over a socket from a front-end web server (nginx/Apache) or, in many misconfigurations, directly from a network peer. The name and value byte streams — and critically their lengths (nameLength/valueLength FastCGI fields, up to 2^31-1 each) — are fully attacker-controllable. Everything reaching this function (the chain contents, the chain length returned by computeChainDataLength(), and even whether the chains are non-null) is hostile and unvalidated at this point.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the member-function definition of FastCGITransport::onHeader, the callback the FastCGI protocol session invokes once per decoded parameter (HTTP header / CGI variable). It takes two owning smart pointers: key_chain, an IOBuf chain holding the raw bytes of the parameter NAME, and value_chain, an IOBuf chain holding the raw bytes of the parameter VALUE. folly::IOBuf is a chained (potentially multi-segment) buffer, so the logical byte string can be split across several linked buffers. Ownership is transferred by value (unique_ptr), meaning onHeader is responsible for the lifetime of both chains for the duration of the call. Both arguments originate from parsing untrusted FastCGI records.

A folly::io::Cursor is constructed over the raw pointer of the key chain (key_chain.get()); a Cursor is a sequential reader that walks the linked IOBuf segments. key_chain->computeChainDataLength() sums the byte lengths of every segment in the chain to compute the total logical size of the name (a size_t). readFixedString(len) then reads exactly that many bytes out of the cursor and materializes them into a std::string named key. Because the requested length equals the chain's own total length, the read consumes the entire name chain into a single contiguous std::string — including any embedded NUL or control bytes, since std::string is length-counted, not NUL-terminated.

The identical pattern is repeated for the parameter VALUE. A Cursor is built over value_chain.get(), the total value length is computed with value_chain->computeChainDataLength(), and readFixedString reads that full length into the std::string value. As with the key, the entire attacker-supplied value byte string is copied verbatim into a heap-backed std::string with no length cap, no character-set validation, and no rejection of control characters or NUL bytes.

The decoded name/value pair is inserted into the transport member m_requestParams, an associative map (name -> value) that accumulates every FastCGI parameter for the current request. operator[] with assignment creates the entry if the key is new or overwrites the existing entry if the key was already present (last-writer-wins). This map is the staging area that HHVM later exposes to the request as its server/environment variables. The function returns, releasing both IOBuf chains (unique_ptr destructors).

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: There is no upper bound on the length of an individual parameter, nor on the aggregate size or count of parameters accumulated in m_requestParams. computeChainDataLength() returns whatever total the FastCGI name/value length fields declared (FastCGI encodes these as up to 31-bit lengths), and readFixedString faithfully allocates a std::string of that size. An attacker (or a compromised/misconfigured front-end) can stream FCGI_PARAMS records declaring enormous names/values, or a very large number of distinct keys, forcing HHVM to allocate the corresponding heap memory eagerly and retain it in m_requestParams for the request's lifetime. This is uncontrolled resource consumption / memory exhaustion leading to denial of service. The invariant the implementation must uphold is a per-parameter length cap and a total-params budget (count and cumulative bytes) enforced BEFORE materializing the strings and inserting them; nothing here enforces that.

The name and value are copied as raw, length-counted byte strings with no validation or normalization (the CWE-665 improper-initialization/validation angle: the request-parameter map is populated from unvalidated decoded input). The bytes may contain embedded NULs, CR/LF (\r\n), '=' , or other control characters. When these keys/values are subsequently turned into CGI/server environment variables and header-like values consumed by the running PHP/Hack application, unsanitized separators can enable environment/variable injection or downstream header ambiguity (e.g. a name containing a delimiter that splits into extra variables, or a value carrying CRLF that is later re-emitted). The safe invariant is that parameter names must be validated against an allowed character set (no NUL, no control/separator characters) and rejected otherwise before insertion; onHeader performs no such validation.

Duplicate parameter names are silently collapsed to last-writer-wins by operator[]. FastCGI does not forbid repeating a name across records, so an attacker can send the same header name twice with different values; whichever arrives last overrides the earlier one with no logging or policy. If any security-relevant server variable (e.g. an auth-related header, DOCUMENT_ROOT, SCRIPT_FILENAME, or a proxy-set trust header) can be supplied or duplicated by the client, this override behavior creates a request-interpretation conflict / header-smuggling primitive between the front-end's view and HHVM's view. The implementation should define and enforce an explicit duplicate policy (reject, first-wins, or documented merge) rather than letting untrusted ordering decide.

key_chain and value_chain are dereferenced via key_chain->computeChainDataLength() / value_chain->computeChainDataLength() with no null check. If the FastCGI decode layer ever invokes onHeader with a moved-from or null unique_ptr (for example an FCGI_PARAMS record that legitimately declares a zero-length value which the decoder represents as a null chain rather than an empty IOBuf), the arrow-operator call dereferences a null pointer and crashes the worker (CWE-476 NULL pointer dereference), a remotely triggerable DoS. The contract the caller/implementation must guarantee is that both chains are non-null non-empty IOBufs; if that cannot be guaranteed, onHeader must handle the null case explicitly.

A zero-length name (computeChainDataLength() == 0) yields an empty std::string key, and m_requestParams[""] = value stores a nameless parameter. This is a minor correctness/hygiene issue rather than a memory-safety one: an empty or degenerate parameter name should be rejected, since it cannot correspond to a valid CGI variable and only serves to consume a map slot or shadow lookups that treat empty specially.

summary: onHeader ingests FastCGI parameter names and values straight off an untrusted wire and stores them in m_requestParams with no length cap, no character validation, no duplicate-key policy, and no null-chain guard. The memory-safety exposure is low (the read length equals the chain's own length, so there is no over-read), but the code is a solid instance of improper validation/initialization of decoded input: the practical risks are uncontrolled memory consumption (DoS), unsanitized name/value bytes flowing into request environment variables (injection/ambiguity downstream), and a possible NULL dereference on a null chain.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point receiving two untrusted IOBuf chains by owning pointer; both are dereferenced without null checks (NULL-deref contract).
Attacker-controlled name chain; its declared length drives an uncapped allocation and its raw bytes are stored unvalidated.
Attacker-controlled value chain; same uncapped-allocation and unvalidated-bytes exposure as the key.
Length is attacker-supplied (up to 31-bit FastCGI nameLength) with no cap; also dereferences key_chain without a null check.
Same uncapped attacker-controlled length and null-deref exposure for the value.
Last-writer-wins insert with no duplicate policy, no key validation, and no bound on the number of entries.

The Cursor read length equals the same chain's computeChainDataLength(), so the read cannot exceed the buffer — no over-read here; the risk lives in the length value and stored bytes, captured by the anchors above.

findings.title: Unbounded FastCGI parameter size/count — uncontrolled resource consumption (DoS)
findings.anchor: readFixedString(computeChainDataLength()) for key and value; insertion into m_requestParams
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0192_cwe665.cpp (onHeader) plus the FastCGI session driver, implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Each parameter name and value must be capped to a sane maximum length, and the total number of parameters and their cumulative byte size must be budgeted, with the check applied BEFORE materializing the std::strings and inserting into m_requestParams.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker streams FCGI_PARAMS records declaring very large name/value lengths, or a very large number of distinct keys. computeChainDataLength() returns the attacker's declared size and readFixedString allocates it; the map retains every entry for the request lifetime. This drives the worker to allocate arbitrary heap memory, causing memory exhaustion and denial of service across concurrent requests.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the FastCGI session enforces per-param length limits and a total params budget (count and bytes) upstream of onHeader; if not, add caps and reject oversized inputs.

findings.title: Unvalidated parameter name/value bytes stored as request variables (improper validation of decoded input)
findings.anchor: key/value built from raw IOBuf bytes then stored in m_requestParams
findings.bug_class: Improper initialization/validation of untrusted input; potential environment/variable injection downstream
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0192_cwe665.cpp (onHeader) and the consumer that promotes m_requestParams to server variables, implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Parameter names must be validated against an allowed character set (reject embedded NUL, CR, LF, '=', and other control/separator bytes) before they are stored and later turned into CGI/server environment variables.
findings.failure_mode: readFixedString copies raw length-counted bytes, so key/value may contain NUL, CRLF, or separator characters. When these are later exposed as $_SERVER/CGI variables or re-emitted, an attacker-crafted name/value can inject extra variables, truncate at a NUL, or introduce header ambiguity in downstream consumers.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether names/values are sanitized or character-class-validated anywhere before m_requestParams is exposed to the request environment; confirm how embedded NUL/CRLF are handled.

findings.title: Duplicate parameter name silently overwritten (last-writer-wins)
findings.bug_class: Interpretation conflict / header override (request smuggling primitive)
findings.invariant: There must be an explicit, documented policy for duplicate parameter names (reject / first-wins / defined merge) so that untrusted ordering cannot silently override a previously-set, possibly security-relevant server variable.
findings.failure_mode: operator[] assignment overwrites any prior value for the same key with no logging or policy. An attacker repeating a header name (e.g. an auth or proxy-trust variable) causes the last occurrence to win, creating a mismatch between the front-end's and HHVM's view of the request and enabling header smuggling/override.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether any earlier layer deduplicates or rejects repeated FastCGI param names, and whether any security decision depends on a param that a client can duplicate.

findings.title: Missing null check on IOBuf chains before dereference
findings.invariant: Either the caller must guarantee key_chain and value_chain are always non-null non-empty IOBufs, or onHeader must null-check both before calling computeChainDataLength()/using the cursor.
findings.failure_mode: If the FastCGI decoder represents a zero-length or absent name/value as a null unique_ptr (or passes a moved-from chain), key_chain->computeChainDataLength() dereferences nullptr and crashes the worker — a remotely triggerable denial of service.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether the FastCGI protocol session can ever invoke onHeader with a null/empty chain for a zero-length param, and whether a guard exists there or is needed here.

findings.title: Empty parameter name accepted
findings.anchor: key derived from a possibly zero-length chain, then used as map key
findings.invariant: A parameter with an empty name should be rejected, since it cannot map to a valid CGI/server variable.
findings.failure_mode: A FastCGI param with nameLength 0 yields key == "" and stores m_requestParams[""] = value; this consumes a map slot and can shadow code that treats the empty key specially, with no legitimate use.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether an empty parameter name is filtered anywhere before or after onHeader.

audit_checklist: Confirm the FastCGI session enforces a per-parameter length cap and a total params count/byte budget BEFORE onHeader materializes strings; add caps if absent.
Confirm parameter names are validated against an allowed character set (reject NUL, CR, LF, '=', control/separator bytes) before being exposed as server variables.
Verify the duplicate-name policy for m_requestParams and whether any security-relevant server variable can be overridden by a repeated client header.
Verify key_chain and value_chain can never be null/moved-from when onHeader is called; add null guards if the decoder can emit them for zero-length params.
Verify empty parameter names are rejected rather than stored under the "" key.
Trace how m_requestParams is later promoted to $_SERVER/CGI environment and confirm no embedded NUL/CRLF byte survives into that promotion.
